rationale: This reaction is a classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction. Tert-butanol is protonated by sulfuric acid and loses water to form a stable tert-butyl carbocation. This carbocation is then attacked by the nitrogen of acetonitrile, forming a nitrilium ion intermediate. Subsequent hydrolysis yields N-tert-butylacetamide. The reactants are simple, acyclic, and clearly demonstrate the core transformation: the conversion of a carbocation source and a nitrile into an N-alkyl amide.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic aliphatic substrates (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) to avoid overlap with reactions involving aromatic rings, complex ring systems, or other functional groups that might trigger different named reaction classifications. The core transformation is clearly the formation of an amide from a carbocation and a nitrile, which is the defining feature of the Ritter reaction.
